revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Izumo-taishakyo]][[fa:ایزومو-تایشاکیو]][[ja:出雲大社教]][[simple:Izumo-taishakyo]]
{{Short description|Japanese Shinto grouping}} {{Infobox organization | name = Izumo Taishakyo or Izumo Oyashirokyo | native_name = 出雲大社教 | native_name_lang = Japanese | image = File:Izumo_Taisha_IMG_20161020_105432.jpg | image_size = 280 | alt = Izumo Taisha Kagura Hall photographed from Izumo Taisha Academic Affairs Headquarters, the headquarters of Izumo Taisha. | caption = Izumo Taisha Kagura Hall photographed from Izumo Taisha Academic Affairs Headquarters, the headquarters of Izumo Taisha | logo = File:USVA headstone emb-34.svg | logo_size = 150 | logo_alt = Izumo-taishakyo Crest | logo_caption = Izumo-taishakyo Crest | founder = [[Senge Takatomi]] | type = Religious Organization | headquarters = [[Izumo-taisha]], {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | language = Japanese | parent_organization = Kyoha Shintō Rengokai | website = http://www.izumooyashirokyo.or.jp }}
{{nihongo|'''Izumo-taishakyo'''|出雲大社教|Izumo taishakyō|in Japanese ''Izumo Ooyashirokyō''<ref>[[:ja:出雲大社教]], corresponding Japanese Wikipedia entry</ref>}} is a Japanese [[Shinto]] grouping. It was established by [[Senge Takatomi]] (1845–1918), the 80th head priest of [[Izumo-taisha]] in 1882, as one of the original thirteen sects of ''Kyoha Shintō Rengokai'' (Association of Sectarian Shinto), during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} in {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}.<ref name="google3">{{cite book|title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language|author=Miller, R.A.|date=1962|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=9780804816472|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC|page=115|accessdate=2015-01-01}}</ref><ref name="google">{{cite book|title=Shinto, a Short History|author1=Teeuwen, M.|author2=Breen, J.|author3=Inoue, N.|author4=Mori, M.|date=2003|publisher=RoutledgeCurzon|isbn=9780415311793|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC|page=177|accessdate=2015-01-01}}</ref>
==Overview== There is a headquarters (Cultural Affairs Office) on the precincts of Izumo Taisha in {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}} and {{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|qid=Q301580|lt=Izumo City|1=Izumo}}, and the staff of Izumo Taisha is also the staff of Izumo-taishakyo. However, while Izumo-taisha itself is affiliated with [[:en:Jinja Honcho|Jinja Honcho]], legally speaking, Izumo-taishakyo is an independent religious corporation separate from Jinja Honcho and associated with Kyoha Shintō Rengokai. Thus Izumo-taishakyo walks a middle ground between [[Shrine Shinto]] and [[Sect Shinto]].<ref name="google2">{{cite book|title=Religions in Japan|author=Japan. Monbushō. Shūmuka|date=1959|publisher=Religious Affairs Section, Research Bureau, Ministry of Education, Government of Japan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WhweAAAAMAAJ|accessdate=2015-01-01}}</ref>
[[File:Takatomi Senge 01.jpg|thumb|300px|[[Senge Takatomi]], the founder of Izumo-taishakyo]] During the Meiji Period, priests from Izumo Taisha were collected to participate in the creation of a centralized {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}. However, due to conflicts primarily with the traditions of [[:en:Ise-jingu|Ise-jingu]], the Izumo tradition was separated into its own sect. In 1872, [[Amaterasu]], [[Takamimusubi]], [[Kamimusubi]], and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] were decided to be the four main Kami for the national religion, but the Izumo faction argued that [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] should also be worshiped, which led to a great debate with the Ise faction. However, the Ise faction gained the support of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}, and the Izumo faction was defeated and Okuninushi was not enshrined. In addition, the "separatist religion decrees" issued by the Meiji government made it so that it was forbidden to carry out the Izumo religion independently of the state. For that reason, Senge Takatomi, the head priest of Izumo-taisha at the time, resigned from Izumo Taisha Omiya and founded Izumo-taishakyo. Izumo-taishakyo was integrated with Izumo-taisha in 1951, but Izumo-taishakyo and Izumo-taisha remain as separate entities.
Under the {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|qid=Q696251|lt=Allied occupation after World War II|1=Occupation of Japan}}, Shinto was separated from the government control and Izumo-taisha was reformed into a private shrine, then Senge and its Izumo-taisha-kyo took back the position of the administrator of Izumo-taisha. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|simple|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. he died in February 2002 at the age of 89.
The predecessor of Izumo-taishakyo was "Izumo Taisha Keijin-ko", which was established in 1873 by Senge Takatomi, to centralize Izumo-ko (an organization that uses Izumo-taisha as a religion shrine) throughout the country. Propagation institutions are set up all over Japan, especially in the west, centered on the [[:en:Chugoku region|Chugoku region]]. Churches (''教会'') exist throughout Japan as well, however, they are not officially affiliated with Izumo-taisha itself, and are merely described as "missionary organizations for the Izumo Taisha religion," so they are not referred to as "shrines" or "branches", and thus are not "shrines" that is an inclusive religious corporation of the central government office, nor is a "branch" that is an inclusive religion corporation of the religious corporation Izumo Taishakyo. "Izumo-taisha Okinawa", which was established in Okinawa during the U.S. occupation, did not receive the name "church" at the time. In addition, there are lay organizations nationwide associated with Izumo-taishakyo known as "Ko".
[[File:IZUMO TAISHA-KYO MISSION 01.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii, Honolulu, Hawaii]] Outside of Japan, Hawaii Izumo Taisha in [[:en:Honolulu, Hawaii|Honolulu]] was founded in 1906 when Rev. Katsuyoshi Miyao opened a temporary {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} on Aala Street near Aala Park on 26 September 1906.<ref>Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 153.</ref> A temporary shrine building was completed on 25 August 1907. A permanent [[honden|shrine building]] was completed in 1922.<ref>Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 161.</ref> By 1941, there were branches of Izumo Taishakyo operating in {{ill|Hilo, Hawaii|en|Hilo, Hawaii|qid=Q216258|lt=Hilo|1=Hilo, Hawaii}}, {{ill|Wailuku, Hawaii|en|Wailuku, Hawaii|qid=Q2087831|lt=Wailuku|1=Wailuku, Hawaii}}, {{ill|Waipahu, Hawaii|en|Waipahu, Hawaii|qid=Q637246|lt=Waipahu|1=Waipahu, Hawaii}}, {{ill|Pearl City, Hawaii|en|Pearl City, Hawaii|qid=Q572052|lt=Pearl City|1=Pearl City, Hawaii}}, [[:en:Honouliuli|Honouliuli]], Ewa Lower Camp, [[:en:Aiea, Hawaii|Aiea]], and [[:en:Kakaako, Hawaii|Kakaako]].<ref>Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 174.</ref>
As of 2014, the Senge family of the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Izumo clan|simple|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} had been the heads of the shrine for the last six generations since Senge Takatomi.<ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140619/rgn1406190070-n1.html 出雲大社教新管長に千家氏が就任] - 産経新聞 2014th year 6月19日</ref>
Hitachi Izumo-taisha Shrine (formerly Izumo-taisha Hitachi Church) was a branch of Izumo Taisha Shrine located in Ibaraki Prefecture, however it has been a separate entity since 2013.<ref>[https://izumotaisha.or.jp/standing/ 我ら立てり] - 常陸国出雲大社</ref>
==Beliefs and doctrine== [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|250px|Okuninushi]] Izumo-taishakyo holds [[Ōkuninushi|Okuninushi]] as the head kami of the faith, as well as holding [[Ame no Hohi|Ame no Hohi]] as an important kami, who is believed to be the ancestor of the {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|simple|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from whom the founder of the faith, Senge Takatomi, as well as all heads of Izumo-taisha, are descended.
Okuninushi is understood as the kami of "''En-musubi''" (lit. The Tying of Bonds) and is considered to preside over all human relationships, such as friendship, marriage, and professional relationships. He is considered by many worshipers of Izumo-taishakyo to be the predominant Kami in Japanese mythology.
In addition, there is the doctrine of {{Nihongo3|Unity of the Obscured and the Visible worlds|幽顕一如|Yuken Ichinyo}} in which it is believed that "the visible world and the invisible world, life and death are two sides of the same coin", and that humans do not come into existence upon birth, nor cease to exist upon death, but that there is a degree of reincarnation where humans inherit a spirit from their ancestors. A person alternates between the physical world and the spiritual world.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.izumo-kobe.jp/cont6/main.html|title = 神葬祭（神道のお葬式）しんそうさい | 神戸のいづもさん 出雲大社 神戸分祠}}</ref> The [[Tokoyo no kuni|Spirit World]] (幽世 ''kakuriyo'') is the domain of Okuninushi, which he was granted domain over after he relinquished control of his physical kingdom to the [[Amatsukami]].<ref>[http://www.izumooyashirokyo.or.jp/300_kyoso-kyogi.html The Founder of Izumo Oyashirokyo] - Izumo Oyashirokyo ''(in Japanese)''</ref>
Unlike the typical practice of Shrine Shinto, but in line with the practice of {{ill|Konkokyo|en|Konkokyo|qid=Q897040|lt=Konkokyo|1=Konkokyo}}, a fellow Sect Shinto faith, during prayers, worshipers bow twice deeply with hands placed around the knees, then clap four times, instead of the more typical two, to gain the attention of the kami.<ref>[https://www.izumotaishahawaii.com/about/rituals-and-symbols Rituals and Symbols] - Izumo Taisha Hawaii</ref> This has been explained as being that the worshipper does not draw attention only to the themselves, but to future partners, such as a future spouse, when praying at Izumo-taisha.<ref>{{cite book |last=Brockman|first=Norman|date=2011|title=Encyclopedia of Sacred Places|url=https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasacr00broc|url-access=limited|page=[https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasacr00broc/page/n277 247]|isbn=978-1-59884-654-6}}</ref>
==See also== {{Commons Category|Izumo-ooyashirokyo Soreisha}} *[[Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii]] *[[Izumo-taisha]]
